content.text: System-Assigned vs User-Assigned Managed Identity

Choosing between system-assigned and user-assigned managed identities is an architectural decision that affects identity lifecycle management, operational complexity, and infrastructure-as-code patterns. Both types eliminate secrets from your code, but they fit different scenarios. This topic helps you pick the right type and migrate between them when requirements change.

attrs.alt: Decision guide: system-assigned for single resources with tied lifecycle; user-assigned for shared identities, stable principal IDs, and fleet scenarios

content.text: Comprehensive Comparison
Dimension
System-Assigned
User-Assigned
Creation
Automatic — created when the resource is enabled
Manual — you create it as a standalone Azure resource
Lifecycle
Deleted when the resource is deleted
Independent — you delete it explicitly
Sharing
Cannot be shared; 1-to-1 with resource
Can be attached to many resources simultaneously
Principal ID stability
New principal ID if resource is recreated
Same principal ID regardless of attached resources
Pre-provisioning
Not possible — identity needs the resource first
Can create identity and assign roles before compute exists
IaC circular dependency
Can cause issues: role assignment needs principal ID at deploy time
Solved: create identity first, then compute; reference identity resource ID
Role assignment count
One per resource (N resources = N role assignments)
One per identity (all attached resources share assignments)
DefaultAzureCredential usage
Automatic selection when only one identity
Must set AZURE_CLIENT_ID when multiple identities attached
Supported resources
Most Azure compute (VM, App Service, Functions, ACI, AKS nodes)
Same compute types; also supported in VMSS templates, AKS workload identity
Cost
No additional cost
No additional cost
Audit trail
SP named after the resource
SP named after the identity resource — persists across compute changes
Scenario Walkthroughs
Scenario 1 — Choose System-Assigned: Single VM Reading a Key Vault
A single VM needs to read one Key Vault secret at startup. The VM will not be recreated (it is a long-lived utility server), and no other resource needs the same access.
Best choice: system-assigned. The lifecycle is tied correctly, no sharing is needed, and the setup is simpler — enable the identity on the VM, assign Key Vault Secrets User, done.

# System-assigned — 2 commands to set up
az vm identity assign --resource-group RG --name MyVM

PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az vm show --resource-group RG --name MyVM \
    --query identity.principalId --output tsv)

KV_ID=$(az keyvault show --name MyVault --query id --output tsv)
az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
    --scope "$KV_ID"
Scenario 2 — Choose User-Assigned: 10-VM Fleet Accessing Storage

A fleet of 10 identical worker VMs all need to read from the same Storage Account. The fleet is managed via a VM Scale Set and scales between 5 and 20 instances. Recreating or scaling instances must not require new role assignments.
Best choice: user-assigned. One identity, one role assignment on the storage account. All 20 (or 5) VMs get access automatically by attaching the identity. No role management on scale events.

# User-assigned — create once, attach to VMSS model
az identity create --resource-group RG --name worker-identity

PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az identity show --resource-group RG --name worker-identity \
    --query principalId --output tsv)
IDENTITY_ID=$(az identity show --resource-group RG --name worker-identity \
    --query id --output tsv)

# One role assignment — applies to all VMSS instances
STORAGE_ID=$(az storage account show --name workerstorage --query id --output tsv)
az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
    --scope "$STORAGE_ID"

# Attach to VMSS — all existing and future instances inherit the identity
az vmss identity assign \
    --resource-group RG \
    --name MyVMSS \
    --identities "$IDENTITY_ID"
Scenario 3 — Choose User-Assigned: IaC With Pre-Provisioning

A Terraform deployment creates a VM and needs to assign it a role before the VM starts — but Terraform cannot create a role assignment until the principal ID exists. With a system-assigned identity, there is a chicken-and-egg problem: the principal ID is only known after deployment. With a user-assigned identity, you create the identity first and reference its known principal ID before the VM exists.
Migrating from System-Assigned to User-Assigned

As a workload grows from a single VM to a fleet, or as IaC complexity increases, you may need to migrate from a system-assigned identity to a user-assigned one. The migration requires creating the new identity, re-assigning all roles, and then swapping the identity configuration on the resource.

# Step 1: Create the new user-assigned identity
az identity create --resource-group RG --name new-shared-identity

NEW_PRINCIPAL=$(az identity show --resource-group RG --name new-shared-identity \
    --query principalId --output tsv)
NEW_IDENTITY_ID=$(az identity show --resource-group RG --name new-shared-identity \
    --query id --output tsv)

# Step 2: Get the current system-assigned principal's role assignments
OLD_PRINCIPAL=$(az vm show --resource-group RG --name MyVM \
    --query identity.principalId --output tsv)

az role assignment list --assignee "$OLD_PRINCIPAL" --output table

# Step 3: Re-create each role assignment on the new identity
# (example: Key Vault Secrets User)
KV_ID=$(az keyvault show --name MyVault --query id --output tsv)
az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$NEW_PRINCIPAL" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
    --scope "$KV_ID"

# Step 4: Attach user-assigned and remove system-assigned
az vm identity assign \
    --resource-group RG \
    --name MyVM \
    --identities "$NEW_IDENTITY_ID"

az vm identity remove \
    --resource-group RG \
    --name MyVM \
    --identities '[system]'

# Step 5: Update app config — set AZURE_CLIENT_ID to new identity's clientId
NEW_CLIENT_ID=$(az identity show --resource-group RG --name new-shared-identity \
    --query clientId --output tsv)
echo "Set AZURE_CLIENT_ID=$NEW_CLIENT_ID in your app settings"
Mixing Both Types on One Resource

A resource can have both a system-assigned identity and one or more user-assigned identities simultaneously. This lets a resource have its own unique identity for some permissions (audited individually) while sharing a fleet identity for shared permissions.

# Enable system-assigned AND attach a user-assigned on the same VM
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'RG' -Name 'MyVM'

$identityId = '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/RG/providers/Microsoft.ManagedIdentity/userAssignedIdentities/shared-identity'

Update-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'RG' -VM $vm `
    -IdentityType 'SystemAssigned, UserAssigned' `
    -IdentityId @($identityId)

# Verify
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'RG' -Name 'MyVM'
Write-Host 'Identity type:' $vm.Identity.Type
Write-Host 'System principal:' $vm.Identity.PrincipalId
Write-Host 'User identities:' ($vm.Identity.UserAssignedIdentities.Keys -join ', ')
Quick Decision Reference
If you need...
Choose
Simplest possible setup for one resource
System-Assigned
Identity shared across 2+ resources
User-Assigned
Same permissions when VM is replaced
User-Assigned
IaC (Terraform/Bicep) without circular dependency
User-Assigned
VM Scale Set or Container group
User-Assigned
AKS pod-level identity (Workload Identity)
User-Assigned
Dev/test single function or web app
System-Assigned
Automatic cleanup when resource is deleted
System-Assigned
Per-resource unique audit trail in Entra ID logs
System-Assigned
Audit trail that persists across compute replacements
User-Assigned
Token Acquisition Differences in Code

At the application code level, both identity types use the same Azure SDK DefaultAzureCredential. The only difference is when a resource has multiple identities — in that case you must disambiguate by passing the client ID of the identity you want.
System-Assigned — No Client ID Needed

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

# System-assigned: one identity, no disambiguation needed
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url='https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net',
    credential=credential
)
# Works as long as the VM/App Service has only one identity
User-Assigned — Specify Client ID When Multiple Identities Exist

import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

# Option A: Set AZURE_CLIENT_ID env var — DefaultAzureCredential picks it up automatically
# az webapp config appsettings set --settings AZURE_CLIENT_ID='<client-id>'
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()   # reads AZURE_CLIENT_ID from environment

# Option B: Explicit ManagedIdentityCredential with client_id
client_id  = os.environ['USER_IDENTITY_CLIENT_ID']
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential(client_id=client_id)

client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url='https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net',
    credential=credential
)
C# — Selecting Identity by Client ID
Verifying Identity Type via Azure REST API

You can programmatically check which identity type is active on a resource using the Azure Resource Manager REST API or the Azure CLI. This is useful in automation scripts that need to verify identity configuration before proceeding.
Governance: Azure Policy for Managed Identity Standards

Azure Policy can enforce which type of managed identity resources in your organization are allowed to use — for example, requiring user-assigned identities on all VMs to prevent orphaned system-assigned service principals.
Audit Current Identity Types Across a Subscription

# List all VMs and their identity type in a subscription
az vm list \
    --query '[].{Name:name, RG:resourceGroup, Identity:identity.type}' \
    --output table

# List all App Service apps and identity type
az webapp list \
    --query '[].{Name:name, RG:resourceGroup, Identity:identity.type}' \
    --output table

# Identify resources with system-assigned identity (for migration planning)
az resource list \
    --query "[?identity.type=='SystemAssigned'].{Name:name, Type:type, RG:resourceGroup}" \
    --output table
KQL: Comparing Audit Footprints

System-assigned identities leave an audit trail in Entra ID sign-in logs under the name of the Azure resource. User-assigned identities appear under the identity's own name, which persists even when resources are replaced. The difference matters for security investigations and compliance reporting.

// Compare sign-in patterns: system-assigned (named after resource) vs
// user-assigned (named after the identity resource)
AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where ServicePrincipalName in (
    'MyVM',                  // system-assigned — named after the VM
    'my-shared-identity'     // user-assigned — named after the identity
  )
| summarize
    Calls     = count(),
    Resources = make_set(ResourceDisplayName),
    LastCall  = max(TimeGenerated)
    by ServicePrincipalName
| order by Calls desc

// Find all managed identity service principals (both types)
// that signed in to a specific resource (e.g., Key Vault)
AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(14d)
| where ResourceDisplayName == 'MyKeyVault'
| where ServicePrincipalType == 'ManagedIdentity'
| summarize
    Calls      = count(),
    Identities = make_set(ServicePrincipalName)
    by ResourceDisplayName, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Cost and Limits
Limit
System-Assigned
User-Assigned
Cost
Free
Free
Max per subscription
Unlimited (1 per resource)
1000 user-assigned identities per subscription
Max per resource
Up to 20 user-assigned identities per resource
Principal ID changes on re-create
Yes — new ID after deletion and recreation
No — stable across all resource lifecycle events
Role assignments that survive resource deletion
No — deleted with the resource
Yes — survive resource deletion
The 1000 user-assigned identity limit per subscription and 20 per resource are soft limits — you can request an increase from Azure Support if your architecture requires more.
Summary
Both identity types eliminate credential management from your code — the choice is purely operational and architectural.
System-assigned: automatic, tied to resource lifecycle, 1-to-1 — best for standalone or simple single-resource workloads.
User-assigned: standalone resource, shareable, stable principal ID, pre-provisionable — best for fleets, IaC, and autoscaling.
Role assignments: system-assigned requires N assignments for N resources; user-assigned requires one assignment regardless of fleet size.
Application code: DefaultAzureCredential works for both; set AZURE_CLIENT_ID when multiple user-assigned identities are attached.
Migration path: create user-assigned, re-create role assignments, attach to resource, remove system-assigned, update AZURE_CLIENT_ID in app config.
Both types support mixing: a resource can simultaneously have system-assigned and one or more user-assigned identities.
